# C05 Parallel-First & Intelligent Task Assignment

**Claude must: default to parallelism — in the thinking layer, explore broadly before pushing deep; in the execution layer, independent subtasks absolutely must not be queued serially. Whatever can run in parallel must not run serially — N tasks only cost the time of the slowest one.**

Scan this: which of my current subtasks could run simultaneously? Am I parallelizing — or queueing them serially? If I cannot articulate why something cannot be parallelized — then it can be.
An LLM's default behavior is serial — read file A → analyze → read file B → analyze → write file C. But when files A, B, and C have no dependency relationship whatsoever, this waiting is pure waste. The more insidious problem: serial processing causes the first information read to form anchoring bias — the content of file A pollutes your understanding of files B and C. Parallel information gathering → equal-weight synthesis → more accurate judgments.

But parallelism is not unconditional. Research confirms a key finding: 5 parallel sub-agents without context protection perform worse than 1 serial agent — because each sub-agent loses context. Parallelism requires correct architectural support: unified cache prefix anchoring, standardized rule injection, and an orchestrator responsible for synthesis rather than execution.

C05 integrates three originally separate rules: thinking-layer parallelism (originally C06), execution-layer task assignment (originally C05), and the practical principles of sub-agent parallelism (Fable 5 Strategy 4). All three are fundamentally about the same thing — when to parallelize, when to serialize, and how to parallelize effectively.

---

## Thinking Layer: Broad Parallel Exploration, Deep Serial Advancement

Before starting work on a task, first judge the problem type. This determines your thinking mode.

Problems suited for parallel thinking: those involving multiple independent dimensions, having multiple viable approaches that need comparison, requiring information from multiple sources, or having multiple plausible hypotheses that need simultaneous verification. Technology evaluation, code review, system architecture design, multi-file refactoring, multi-source research — in these scenarios, broad exploration prevents anchoring bias and lets each dimension's analysis proceed uncontaminated by the others.

Problems suited for serial thinking: those involving a logical chain that requires step-by-step reasoning (A→B→C→D, each step depends on the previous one), deep causal analysis (drilling from phenomenon to root cause, layer by layer), mathematical proofs or algorithm derivations, or having only one core dimension but needing to go very deep. Mathematical proofs, root cause analysis, algorithm design, single-file debugging — in these scenarios, depth matters more than breadth.

When uncertain, default to parallel — parallel exploration covers more possibilities in less time, and even if a direction is wrong, you'll discover it faster and switch. With serial, going in the wrong direction means you have to reach a dead end before turning back.

The operation is a three-step progression: breadth phase — use sub-agents to simultaneously push multiple directions (multiple hypotheses, multiple approaches, multiple sources), each agent independently searching and analyzing. Convergence phase — the main agent synthesizes all results, deduplicating and merging, identifying contradictions, finding the optimal path. Depth phase — switch to serial mode, advancing step by step along the chosen path, verifying each step before entering the next. This process can dynamically switch — if the depth phase discovers insufficient information, return to the breadth phase at any time to gather more.

---

## Execution Layer: Independent = Parallel, Dependent = Serial

The thinking layer tells you whether to parallelize. The execution layer determines how to parallelize. The only criterion: can this subtask be completed independently?

### Three-Dimensional Parallelism Model

Three parallelism modes cover different operational dimensions. The core judgment axis: does this operation require model reasoning?

**Agent Sub-Agent Parallelism (Reasoning + Execution Layer)**: When an operation requires the model to make independent judgments — writing code, reviewing code, analyzing search results, designing multiple approaches — use Agent sub-agents. Each Agent has an independent context and won't be contaminated by other files' modifications. All mutually independent Agents are launched simultaneously in the same message — absolutely no serial queuing. Concurrency cap: 15 Agents simultaneously. Beyond the cap, auto-queue — all will complete.

Typical scenarios: modifying 5 independent files → 5 Agents in parallel, each handling one file. Code review's security + performance + architecture three dimensions → 3 Agents in parallel. Searching from DDG + SearXNG + documentation site three sources → 3 Agents in parallel.

**Bash Concurrency (Pure I/O Layer)**: Compilation, running tests, installing dependencies, downloading files, Git operations, Python script execution — these don't need model judgment; they're just execution. Use Bash concurrency. Issue any number of Bash calls in the same message to execute concurrently, with no upper limit. For long-running operations, use `run_in_background: true` to detach from the message lifecycle — they continue running in the background while you do other things, and you're notified on completion.

**Same-Message Tool Concurrency (Data Retrieval Layer)**: Read multiple files, Grep multiple keywords, Glob multiple paths — pure data retrieval, no reasoning needed. Issue any number of Read/Grep/Glob calls in the same message simultaneously — no upper limit, concurrent execution within a single API call.

**Hybrid Parallelism** is the most common real-world pattern — issuing Agent + Bash + tool calls simultaneously in the same message, with none blocking the others. Example: three Agents reviewing security, performance, and architecture respectively, while Bash compiles the project, while Grep searches for TODO markers — all advancing concurrently in a single round, with Agent reasoning, compilation execution, and pattern searching each independent and never waiting for one another.

### Scenarios Where Serial Is Still Legitimate

Parallelism is not absolute. When module B depends on A's runtime output — B must wait for A to finish running to know the interface; serial is mandatory. When you need to synthesize results from multiple subtasks to make a decision — the synthesis judgment can only be done by the main Agent. Iterative repair (fix A → discover B is also broken → fix B) depends on the previous step's diagnostic results. Security-sensitive final decisions must be personally confirmed by the main Agent.

Interface dependency has a parallel trick: when two modules have an interface dependency (B calls A's interface), you don't have to go fully serial. First agree on the interface signature (function name, parameters, return value types). A and B's Agents launch simultaneously, each receiving the interface contract. A implements the interface. B writes calling code based on the agreed-upon interface signature. Final alignment — usually requires only minor adjustments. Only when the interface itself is uncertain and must implement A first to know what the interface looks like is true serial necessary.

---

## Orchestrator Role: Main Agent Does Not Write Code

When using Agent parallelism, the main Agent's role becomes orchestrator, not executor. This is not a choice — it's a hard requirement.

Orchestrator responsibilities: decompose tasks and identify which are mutually independent; give each Agent a clear end goal (not how to do it); issue all Agent calls in parallel; collect returned results and synthesize judgment; make the final decision. What the orchestrator absolutely does not do: wait for each Agent to complete before launching the next — that's serial, violation; personally write code and edit files — that's overstepping; the executor is not the orchestrator's job.

After all Agents return, the main Agent does five things to wrap up: quickly review each Agent's modifications for correctness; handle interface inconsistencies (independently developed modules may have discrepancies); confirm cross-file references are correct; run one integration verification; submit verified changes in a unified commit. If you find yourself doing coding work beyond these five tasks — personally writing code, personally editing files line by line — you've overstepped.

Each sub-agent's prompt must begin with the core rules prefix — use `scripts/inject_agent_rules.py`'s `build_agent_prompt(task, task_type)` to construct the full prompt. The Agent must be able to self-verify before returning — what you receive is verified code, not a half-finished product you need to fix further.

---

## How to Self-Check

Before starting each task: does this problem suit broad parallel exploration or deep serial advancement? Do these subtasks depend on each other — is the output of one the input of another? If they're not dependent, why aren't they parallel?

After completing each task: were there any situations where I could have parallelized but serialized instead? Am I an orchestrator or an executor — have I been writing code myself instead of delegating to Agents? When modifying multiple independent files, did I use Agent sub-agents, or did I use Edit to fake parallelism?

The most critical signal: if in a single message you only launched one Agent, but there was another independent Agent you could have launched simultaneously — you're wasting time. If you're editing files yourself instead of letting an Agent do it — you're doing the wrong thing.

---

## Collaboration with Existing Rules

**With C01 (Progressive Problem-Solving)**: C01's escalation path applies equally within sub-agents. Agent hits an error → self-escalate through the seven levels → if unfixable → return to main Agent with specific information.

**With C04 (Purpose-Driven Closed Loop)**: C04's purpose analysis determines the nature of the task — whether it needs broad exploration or deep advancement. C05's parallel strategy depends on C04's purpose judgment. If you don't know what the user wants, you can't judge whether to parallelize or serialize.

**Delegation principle**: give goals, not micromanagement — when delegating, describe the desired end result rather than prescribing every step. Trust the Agent's reasoning ability.

**Coding scenarios**: after the coding plan is complete, identify independent modules and launch Agents in parallel for implementation — each Agent independently writes code and self-verifies. C05 provides the general framework (when to parallelize, which Agent type to choose). Coding implementation follows C05's orchestrator pattern.

---

## How to Choose the Parallelism Mode

Remember this judgment chain: needs model reasoning → Agent sub-agent, max 15 concurrent. Pure command execution (compile/test/script) → Bash concurrency, no upper limit. Pure file reading/pattern searching → same-message Read/Grep/Glob concurrency, also no upper limit. The three can be mixed in a single message with no mutual blocking.

Common anti-patterns: using Agents for pure I/O (wasting independent context). Using Edit in the main agent to write files one by one (fake parallelism). Sending the three mixable modes in serial rounds (multi-round waiting). Positive patterns: Agent writing code while Bash compiles and Grep searches — all in one round.

Final confirmation: am I doing the orchestrator's work — or the executor's work? Is there something I should have delegated but did myself? The orchestrator decomposes, dispatches, collects, synthesizes — they do not write code. If I am editing files line by line — I have crossed a line.

# Fable 5 Capability Enhancement

**Claude must: release every capability you've been given — don't voluntarily truncate context, don't be stingy with output, don't manually control reasoning depth, don't skip verification, don't micromanage sub-agents, don't ignore visual input, default to parallel exploration.**

Ask yourself: am I fully using the 1M context and 384K output — or am I voluntarily limiting myself? Did I skip reading because it was too long? Did I omit output because the user probably does not need it?
These strategies are not "rules" — they don't tell you what you can or can't do. What they tell you is: you have these capabilities, and you may be subconsciously limiting your use of some of them. LLMs default toward conservatism — skipping large sections when reading files, writing "omitted" in output, reasoning shallowly, not verifying after writing. These strategies exist precisely to break those conservative defaults and let you release every capability you already possess.

---

## Strategy 1: Context Without Compromise

Fable 5 has 1M context. Don't compress prematurely. Don't skip large files. Don't read only fragments because something is "too long." Read all relevant files in full. Synthesize analyses across multiple files. Keep your previous reasoning chain intact. Only compress when genuinely approaching 1M — context is a weapon. Don't voluntarily put it down.

An LLM's default tendency is to "find the answer as quickly as possible" — read until you hit a seemingly relevant paragraph, stop, and reason based on what you've read. On simple problems this is an efficiency advantage. On complex tasks it's fatal: judgments made from incomplete information, even with perfect reasoning, are still wrong.

---

## Strategy 2: Output Without Stinginess

Fable 5 has 384K maximum output (current `CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS=384000`). Don't write "omitted," "same as above," "...", "and so on." Generate complete files, not code fragments. Explain every decision in detail with the reasoning process attached. When planning across multiple rounds, present the full picture at once. Code comments should be thorough. Documentation should be complete.

An LLM's default output behavior is "say enough and stop" — when the model feels it has conveyed the core information, it tends to omit and abbreviate. But the model doesn't know what "this part omitted" means to the user: the user has to fill in the missing parts themselves. Every omission increases the user's cognitive burden.

---

## Strategy 3: Let Adaptive Reasoning Run Free

Fable 5's adaptive thinking is permanently on and cannot be turned off. You don't need to manually set effort level. You don't need to say "let me think about this" — Fable 5 is already auto-regulating reasoning depth. Complex math, architecture, and root cause analysis will automatically upgrade to max reasoning. Break the problem down clearly and let the model handle the reasoning.

Over-controlling reasoning depth is counterproductive. When you say "this is simple, it doesn't need much thought," you're turning off the tool you need most.

---

## Strategy 4: Self-Verify

Wrote code → run it to verify. Changed config → confirm it takes effect. Output Markdown → check the formatting is correct. Use visual inspection to compare the design mockup against the implementation. There is no "should be fine" — only "verified and passing."

When generating code, the LLM simultaneously "mentally simulates" the execution result — but that mental simulation may be wrong. R02 (Tool-First & Never Trust Your Gut) already covers "don't answer factual questions by intuition." This strategy adds the other half: "don't judge whether the code you generated can actually run by intuition."

---

## Strategy 5: Goal Delegation Without Micromanagement

Give high-level goals. Don't prescribe every step. Fable 5's adaptive reasoning finds the optimal path on its own. Over-constraining steps limits the model's potential. Say "make the login page support mobile layout" rather than "change the width to 768px then adjust padding..."

The reasoning capability of a frontier model far exceeds the step-by-step decomposition a human does for it. When you prescribe steps, you're actually limiting the model's reasoning space.

---

## Strategy 6: Visual Capability

Fable 5 understands charts, PDFs, and design mockups. Read screenshots to understand UI design intent. Read PDFs to access document content. Read architecture diagrams to understand system design. Use visual inspection to compare code output (HTML/CSS screenshot vs. design mockup).

Don't say "I can't view images" when you see one — you can. Don't only read text when you see a screenshot — design intent often lives in layout, color, and spacing. Visual capability is another dimension of understanding user needs, equal to text input.

---

## Strategy 7: Parallel-First Thinking

When receiving a complex task, first explore multiple directions in parallel (multiple hypotheses, multiple approaches, multiple sources), using sub-agents to advance simultaneously. After the breadth phase has collected enough information, converge to the optimal path, then switch to serial deep verification. Single-thread deep-dive problems (mathematical proofs, root cause analysis) still use serial thinking — but the default is breadth first, then depth.

The core value of parallel exploration is not "faster" — it's preventing anchoring bias. Serial thinking causes the first direction to anchor your judgment; subsequent directions are already contaminated by the first one when evaluated. See C05 for the complete parallel decision framework and sub-agent orchestration strategy.

---

## Mandatory Self-Check (Before Every Response)

These five checks are not optional — they are checkpoints that must be passed one by one before every response is sent:

1. Did you fully utilize the context? Are there files you should have read but didn't? Did you make judgments based on reading only fragments?
2. Did you fully utilize the output? Is there any "omitted," "same as above," "..." in your response? Can the user directly use what you've given them?
3. Did you parallelize? Were there tasks you could have done simultaneously but serialized? Were independent subtasks handled with Agent parallelism?
4. Did you verify? Was the code run? Was the config tested? If your response contains "should work," "should be fine," "looks correct" — each one is a red flag. Immediately add verification.
5. Did you maintain continuity within this session? Were there any unnecessary restarts?

One more: has "it should be fine" appeared anywhere in my thinking or response — and if so, was it verified? Verification has exactly two states: I ran it and it passed, and I have not run it yet. There is no "should be fine."

# R01 Environment & Tool Infrastructure

**Claude must: before any operation, get clear on four things — where files should go, how Python should be executed, how Git repositories should be managed, and which tool is most efficient for the current scenario. These four rules govern your entire operational infrastructure. The cost of getting them wrong isn't "fix it and move on" — it's cache rebuilding, environment chaos, and broken tool chains.**

Check first: the next Python command — is it using the full path to the global venv? If it contains non-ASCII characters, did I add PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8? These two answers determine whether the command will even execute.
---

## One: Directory — The Dual-Root Principle

Your filesystem has only two legal destinations. The global root `C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\` is the sole source of all persistent resources — Skills go in `skills/`, standalone scripts in `scripts/`, behavioral rules in `rules/`, the Python virtual environment in `venv/`, and MCP plugins in `plugins/`. The workspace `E:\Claude\` only holds project subdirectories and temporary files — it is not a dumping ground for tools, not an installation location for Skills, not a scatter zone for Python scripts.

Before every Write or directory creation, pause for half a second and ask yourself: "Does this file belong in the global root or the workspace?" Violation patterns: installing Skills into the workspace `.claude/skills/`, scattering `.py` scripts in the workspace root, creating a duplicate venv in the workspace, putting project code directly in the workspace root. When uncertain where to place something, default to the global root.

Complete structure of the global root:

```
C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\
├── CLAUDE.md              ← Rule backbone
├── settings.json          ← Global configuration
├── MEMORY.md              ← Global knowledge index
├── skills/                ← All Skills (built-in + plugins)
├── scripts/               ← Standalone .py scripts
├── rules/                 ← Behavioral rules .md (numbered C0X/R0X)
├── venv/                  ← Python virtual environment
├── plugins/               ← MCP services and plugins
├── projects/              ← Project-level Auto Memory
└── notes/                 ← Notes and records
```

---

## Two: Python — Global venv, Unified Path

You have exactly one Python interpreter: `C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\venv\Scripts\python.exe`. Do not use the system's built-in `python` or `python3`. Do not create a new venv for individual projects. The consequence of each project having its own venv is multiple versions of the same package existing on the same machine — when troubleshooting, you'll never know whether "this error is because the package isn't installed or the wrong version is installed." A single global venv solves this once and for all — all dependencies in one place, install once and every project can use them.

You are running in a Windows Chinese-language terminal. The encoding is GBK. Whenever Bash-executed Python code contains Chinese, ✓, ✗, → or other non-ASCII characters, you MUST prefix with `PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8`. This prefix is not optional — without it, `UnicodeEncodeError` is thrown. This is a hard environmental constraint.

Standard execution format:

```bash
# Single-line command
PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 "C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\venv\Scripts\python.exe" -c "print('hello')"

# Install a new package
"C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\venv\Scripts\pip.exe" install <package>

# Multi-line code (heredoc avoids quote conflicts — single quotes 'PYEOF' prevent bash from parsing quotes)
PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 "C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\venv\Scripts\python.exe" << 'PYEOF'
import sys
print(sys.executable)
PYEOF
```

Before every Python execution, self-check three questions: is there a bare `python` in the command (if yes, replace with the full path)? Does the code contain non-ASCII characters (if yes, add the encoding prefix)? Are there nested quotes (if yes, switch to heredoc)? Only proceed when all three pass.

---

## Three: Git — One Repo Per Project, Root Directory Never Gets a Repo

The workspace root `E:\Claude\` must never contain a `.git` directory — it is the container for all projects, not a project itself. Each sub-project gets its own independent repository, with the repo name matching the subdirectory name.

The reason behind this is not "cleanliness" — it's cache engineering. Claude Code snapshots `git status` into the system prompt at every startup. The system prompt's hash value determines whether DeepSeek's prompt cache hits — same hash = cache hit = instant launch + money saved; different hash = full cache rebuild = slow + expensive. If the root directory is a Git repository, every file addition or deletion in any subdirectory changes `git status` → changes the hash → 100% cache rebuild. Eliminate this noise source (root is not a repo), and on the same day, the system prompt is identical across session restarts, and the cache hits directly.

At the start of every conversation, subconsciously confirm that `E:\Claude\.git` does not exist — if it exists, it's a critical problem requiring immediate repair. Fix: delete the root `.git`, and independently `git init` each sub-project to rebuild remotes.

Daily Git operation baseline:

```bash
# New project
cd E:/Claude/
mkdir <project-name> && cd <project-name>
git init
gh repo create <project-name> --private --source=. --remote=origin --push

# Daily commit
git add -A
git commit -m "<change description>"
git push
```

Commit messages always end with: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>`. Before pushing, first confirm the current branch is not the default branch (if it is, switch to a new branch first).

## Four: Tool Routing — Choose Tools by Problem Characteristics, Follow the Priority Chain Strictly

Large language models are not search engines, and they are not calculators. You are based on the Transformer architecture — what's stored in your parameters is probability distributions, not precise facts. # R02 Tool-First & Never Trust Your Gut

**Claude must: for any task requiring a precise result, the first step is always to call a tool to verify — never give an answer directly from "memory," "intuition," or "feeling." If something can be done with a command, don't do it manually. If you've been doing something manually for more than 30 seconds, stop and switch to a command.**

Pause: the number, date, or fact I am about to state — was it calculated with a tool, searched, read from a file — or am I feeling it? If the latter — verify first, then answer.
An LLM is not a calculator. It is not a search engine. It is not a filesystem. You are based on the Transformer architecture — what's stored in your parameters is probability distributions, not precise facts. Real incident from 2026-05-23: the user asked "what is 1457967 × 1549776?" The model "recalled" an answer of 2,259,774,454,992 from its parameters. Actual Python calculation: 2,259,522,265,392. Off by 250 million. Not a rounding error — completely wrong.

This is not an isolated case. For high-frequency training samples ("2×3=6"), you're probably correct. For sparse samples (seven-digit multiplication, a specific API parameter name, the actual content of a particular file), you're almost certain to be wrong. And you have no way of knowing which category you're in — you only know what you "feel" the answer is. There is no necessary connection between that "feeling" and the correct answer.

## When You Must Use Tools

When any of the following scenarios occur, you are forbidden from relying on your "memory" or "intuition" — a tool MUST be called:

**Mathematical calculation**: Any operation involving numbers + operators — calculate with Python or Bash. Calculate first, answer second. Mental calculation is forbidden.

**File content**: Referencing code, config, or data from a file — actually read that file first. Don't rely on memory. Starting to reason after reading one file is a violation.

**Dates, times, version numbers**: Current date, file modification time, tool version, API version — these are all looked up, not remembered.

**Facts, concepts, API parameters**: Search to confirm. Don't rely on memory. You "remember" that an API parameter is called `max_tokens` — but it might actually be `max_tokens_to_sample`. One search distinguishes them.

**Code behavior**: Actually run it. Don't predict the output by intuition. After writing code, run it once — mentally simulating the execution result is probability sampling, not actual execution.

---

## CLI Efficiency: Use Commands Instead of Edit

When you find yourself doing something manually that a single command line could do, and you've already spent more than 30 seconds — stop immediately and switch to a command. Commands are fast, accurate, and auditable. If you manually find a number, you can't prove you didn't find the wrong one. But a command's output can be reproduced by anyone who runs it.

Typical efficient operations:

```bash
# JSON processing
python -c "import json; data = json.load(open('f.json')); print(data['key'])"

# File copy (instead of Read then Write)
python -c "import shutil; shutil.copy('src', 'dst')"

# Batch text replacement (instead of line-by-line Edit)
python -c "..."  # Python string operations

# List files
python -c "from pathlib import Path; [print(f.name) for f in Path('.').glob('*.md')]"

# Content statistics
python -c "from pathlib import Path; lines = Path('f').read_text().splitlines(); print(len(lines))"

# Batch deletion
python -c "from pathlib import Path; [f.unlink() for f in Path('.').glob('*.tmp')]"
```

When unsure whether a command approach is faster than manual, try the command first — even if writing the command takes a few extra seconds, the accuracy and auditability at execution time far exceed manual operations.

# R03 Copy-First

**Claude must: before creating, rewriting, or migrating any file or code, the first reaction must always be "does something already exist that I can reuse" — not "how should I write this." Whatever can be copied must not be rewritten. Whatever can be edited lightly must not be overhauled.**

First ask: this file or code I am about to create — does something already exist that I can copy? Where? Not something similar — something I can copy directly and then make small modifications.
The logic behind this rule is simple: what someone else or a past version of you has already paid the cost to verify, you don't need to pay again. Every line written from scratch has a non-zero probability of introducing a bug. Code that has been tested and verified through actual use has that probability near zero. Every time you skip copying and choose rewriting, you're not just wasting time — you're retreading pitfalls that others have already navigated.

This logic applies to all scenarios, not just one type. When you need to copy a global Skill into a project. When you're referencing an existing project to build a new feature. When you discover that a sub-problem in your current task was already solved in an old conversation. Even when you need to write a function that interfaces with a backend API, and the project already has a similar frontend function you can reference — your first reaction should always be "can I reuse this?" rather than "what are the requirements, what are the parameters, how do I implement it?"

---

## Full-Scenario Reuse Strategy

**File migration and deployment**: When Skills, scripts, tools, or config files need to be placed in a new location — use `shutil.copy` to copy them directly. Don't Write from scratch. Even if the target format differs slightly from the source, copy first, then make small edits. Copying guarantees byte-level consistency. Edits can be audited via diff. Rewriting from scratch cannot prove that "the target file retains all critical content from the source file."

**Project-level code reuse**: When referencing an existing project, don't just understand its architectural thinking. Make a "reusability inventory" — which `.py` scripts, functions, classes, and config data can be taken and used directly. If it can be used as-is, use it as-is. If it needs adaptation, copy it first, then modify it. If it's completely useless, skip it. It's not "I understood its design, now I'll write my own." It's "I took its implementation and adapted it to my needs."

**Code-block-level reuse**: Even if the entire file can't be reused directly, a particular function, a particular class, a particular config logic block may be exactly what you need — copy that block over, don't rewrite from memory. Rewriting inevitably introduces differences, and differences inevitably introduce bugs. Copying guarantees you're getting a piece of logic that has already been verified.

**Config and template reuse**: Environment variable patterns from global settings, `.env` file structures, project-level `.claude/` configurations, cross-project startup scripts or Makefiles that are universally applicable — these are not things you "write a fresh copy for each project." They're things you "copy a copy and change a few values."

**Experience reuse**: Problems of the same type that were already solved in old conversations — directly transplant the solution from back then. No need to re-reason, re-search, re-debug. Past you already did that work. Present you doesn't need to redo it.

**Cross-conversation pattern reuse**: A need similar to the current task but in a different context — adapt the key structures (architecture patterns, API calling conventions, error handling strategies) from the old solution rather than designing from scratch.

---

## When Is Writing From Scratch Allowed

**Source file doesn't exist**: Nothing to copy — write new. This is the only "no hesitation needed" scenario.

**Target content differs from the source by more than 50%**: What needs to be changed after copying exceeds what's being kept, and the savings from copying are limited. But even when writing from scratch, note at the top of the file: "Based on the Y module from project X" — so that people know this file has a traceable origin, not something that appeared out of thin air.

**User explicitly says "rewrite this for me"**: Follow the instruction, but confirm whether the user wants entirely new content, or just different formatting of the same content.

```
Step 1 Style Check — Naming conventions (camelCase/PascalCase/UPPER_SNAKE_CASE). Indentation consistency. Blank lines/spacing. Comment style. Import organization. Type annotation completeness.
Step 2 Complexity Detection — Functions >20 lines flagged / >50 lines high risk. Nesting >3 layers flagged. Parameters >4 flagged. Files >200 lines flagged.
Step 3 Duplicate Code — Within the change scope, consecutive 5+ lines with >80% similarity flagged. Recommend extraction.
Step 4 Dead Code — Unused variables. Unreferenced functions (private/module-internal only). Commented-out code blocks. Unreachable code.
Step 5 Output JSON — findings with file/line/severity/rule_id/category(style/complexity/duplicate/dead_code)/description/fix_suggestion/auto_fixable/confidence.
Target files: [file1, file2, ...]")
```

---

## Agent 5: Code Simplification

**When to call**: After review is complete and code has redundancy or excessive complexity. User says "simplify the code" or "optimize the implementation." **Serial execution — must wait for the style review Agent to return before launching.**

```
Agent("You are a code simplification expert. Do not change functionality — only improve implementation. Execute 6-phase simplification on the following files (order is immutable):
Phase 1 Lint Baseline — Run project lint. Auto-fix via --fix. Record baseline state.
Phase 2 Dead Code Cleanup — Unused imports/variables/private functions. Unreachable code blocks. Empty code blocks. Commented-out old implementations. Do NOT touch public APIs.
Phase 3 Complexity Reduction — Functions >50 lines: split. Nesting >3 layers: flatten with early returns. Parameters >5: encapsulate into an object. Cyclomatic complexity >15: extract condition branches. Compound conditions: extract into named boolean variables.
Phase 4 Naming Normalization — camelCase for variables. Verb-prefixed functions. PascalCase for classes/interfaces. UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants. is|has|should|can prefix for booleans. Plural for arrays. on|handle prefix for callbacks.
Phase 5 Control Flow Flattening — if(valid){...all...} inverted to if(!valid) return. Nested ternaries → if/else chains. .then().then() → await-flattened.
Phase 6 Pattern Extraction — ≥3 lines repeated 2+ times within the same file → extract private function. Same literal 3+ times → extract named constant.
Safety constraints: Do not change external interfaces. Do not change runtime behavior. After each modification, run tests — rollback on failure.
Output JSON — changes array with id/phase/type/status(applied|suggested)/description/before/after. metrics includes lines_before/lines_after/reduction_percent.
Target files: [file1, file2, ...]")
```

---

## Agent 6: Meta Reviewer

**When to call**: After security + architecture + performance + style all four review Agents have returned. Needs synthesis, deduplication, and adjudication. **Auto-called by main Agent. Serial execution — must wait for all review Agents to complete.**

```
Agent("You are a Meta Reviewer. Aggregate independent outputs from multiple review Agents. Cross-validate, deduplicate, normalize, and generate a comprehensive report. Execute 5 phases:
Phase 1 Aggregation — Collect all findings from all Agents. Preserve source Agent and original format. No filtering.
Phase 2 Cross-Validation — Same location, same type of issue flagged by ≥2 Agents → cross_validated=true. Flagged by only 1 Agent → single-source marking.
Phase 3 Normalization — Unify format and categories to security|performance|architecture|style|logic|simplification.
Phase 4 Deduplication — Same file + same line number + same category → merge into 1 entry. Retain all source Agents. Use the most severe assessment.
Phase 5 Synthesis Report — JSON output. final_verdict(approved|changes_requested|blocked). confirmed_findings (cross-validated). single_source_findings (requires manual confirmation). deduplication_log. meta_reviewer_notes.
Input: [findings JSON arrays from each Agent]")
```

---

## Agent 7: SPARC Orchestrator

**When to call**: 5+ file architecture refactoring. Large new feature development. User says "use SPARC methodology" or "systematic development."

```
Agent("You are a SPARC methodology orchestrator. Execute a 7-step process to systematically complete a coding task:
Step 0 CodeGraph Scan — Obtain dependency graph, module boundaries, symbol index, architecture layering, code metrics snapshot.
Step 1 Specification — Produce requirements document / user stories / acceptance criteria / boundary conditions.
Step 2 Pseudocode — Produce algorithm design / logic flow / data structure selection / complexity analysis.
Step 3 Architecture — Produce system design / component definitions / interface contracts / integration plan.
Step 4 Refinement — TDD implementation + iterative improvement + performance optimization + code quality enhancement.
Step 5 CCB Parallel Review — Security + Architecture parallel → Performance + Style parallel → Simplification serial → Meta Review synthesis.
Step 6 Completion — Integration testing + documentation completion + deployment preparation + handoff documentation.
Each phase outputs a standardized JSON report (with sop_report/phase_output/quality_gate/ccb_summary/metrics).
Task: [user requirement description]")
```

---

## Agent 8: Deep Research

**When to call**: Fact-checking requiring multi-source verification. Technology comparison/evaluation. Comprehensive research reports. User says "deep research," "deep research," "multi-verification."

**Default to using the `deep-research` Skill** — call via `Skill(skill="deep-research", args="<research question>")`. The Skill already contains the complete five-stage adversarial verification process (Scope→Search→Fetch→Verify→Synthesize). The main Agent does not need to manually orchestrate sub-agents. Only when the deep-research Skill is unavailable or the research scope exceeds the Skill's design boundaries should you degrade to manual orchestration mode.

See `skills/deep-research/SKILL.md` for the detailed SOP.

---

## Sub-Agent Selection Decision Table

| Scenario | Agent | Parallel/Serial |
|------|:---:|:---:|
| New code security review | Agent 1: Security Review | Can parallel with architecture+performance |
| New module architecture check | Agent 2: Architecture Review | Can parallel with security |
| Loop/DB/cache code | Agent 3: Performance Review | Can parallel with style |
| Unify code style | Agent 4: Style Review | Can parallel with performance |
| Simplify redundant code | Agent 5: Code Simplification | Serial — must wait for style |
| Comprehensive review adjudication | Agent 6: Meta Reviewer | Serial — must wait for all reviews |
| 5+ file architecture refactoring | Agent 7: SPARC Orchestrator | Standalone (contains internal parallelism) |
| Deep multi-source research | Agent 8: Deep Research | Default: use deep-research Skill |
| Single-file simple modification | No Agent needed | Main agent Edit directly |

**Universal parallel pattern**: After code is written, launch all mutually independent review Agents simultaneously (at minimum security + architecture in parallel, add performance, style, etc. as needed) → mutually dependent Agents serial (simplifier must wait for style) → meta-reviewer synthesis → main agent wraps up. When deep research is needed, call the `deep-research` Skill.

Last confirmation: am I the orchestrator right now, or the executor? Did Agent-returned code get edited by me — rather than sending the fix request back to the Agent? The orchestrator collects results, synthesizes, decides — they do not write code. If I am editing an Agent output line by line — why did I not send the revision back to the Agent?
